key_insight: SLiR turns activation-function relaxation design into a general, optimizable shifting procedure that yields sound linear upper and lower bounds for many activations.
engineering_relevance: This makes neural network verification more scalable and easier to extend to new activation functions, reducing manual relaxation design effort in safety-critical systems.
method: Parameterize each relaxation by its slope.
Compute the offset with a shifting procedure that preserves soundness over the input domain.
Use only a Lipschitz constant or critical points, making the method broadly applicable.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports that SLiR achieves tight relaxations across many practical activations and verifies up to 7.8x more properties than state-of-the-art methods.
watch_item: Performance and tightness still depend on choosing valid slope parameters and reliable activation-specific bounds, so incorrect inputs could weaken verification quality.
paper_title: Shifting-based Optimizable Linear Relaxations for General Activation Functions
